title: FPN in Object Detection
concept: FPN routes object predictions to specific pyramid levels based on scale, sharing prediction weights across levels.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does semantic transfer resolve shape ambiguity in early layers?

options.text: By sharing deep context to help identify what the local shape represents
By doubling the channel width of the input

exercises.explain: Correct! Deep context guides early layers to correctly classify ambiguous local textures.

cards.title: The Split Prediction
cards.body: Now that FPN has created a pyramid of multi-scale feature maps, how do we actually use them to predict bounding boxes? Do we combine them back into one layer, or do something smarter?

cards.title: Pyramid Prediction Heads
cards.body: FPN attaches independent prediction heads (bounding box regressors and class predictors) to every level of the pyramid (P2, P3, P4, P5, P6). Small objects are predicted at high-res lower levels, and large objects at low-res upper levels.

cards.title: The Post Office Sorter
cards.body: Imagine sorting mail. You have separate slots: a small slot for envelopes (low level, high-res), a medium slot for packages (mid-level), and a large bin for boxes (high level, low-res). FCOS or Faster R-CNN with FPN does the same with object sizes.

cards.title: Multi-Level Inference
cards.body: A street photo contains a massive bus and a tiny pedestrian. FPN predicts the bus bounding box from the P5 layer (7x7) and the pedestrian bounding box from the P2 layer (56x56) simultaneously.

cards.title: Five Times More Weights?
cards.body: You might think tapping into 5 layers means we need 5 different sets of weights for the heads, making the model huge. Fact: FPN shares the same prediction head weights across all pyramid levels, keeping parameters low.

cards.title: Pyramid Scale Mapping
cards.body: How FPN distributes predictions by scale:

left.text:
Resolution: e.g. 56x56
Responsible for small objects
Detects details like keys, birds

right.text:
Resolution: e.g. 7x7
Responsible for large objects
Detects objects like trucks, houses

exercises.prompt: How are predictions distributed across the levels of an FPN pyramid?

options.text: Small objects are predicted at lower, high-resolution levels, while large objects are predicted at upper levels
All objects are predicted only from the top level
Every object is predicted from all levels simultaneously and averaged
The model randomly assigns objects to levels

exercises.explain: Correct! FPN assigns objects to levels based on their size.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To save memory, FPN-based detectors share the same convolutional weight parameters across all prediction heads in the pyramid.
exercises.explain: True. Weight sharing keeps the model parameter count low while enabling multi-scale predictions.

exercises.sentence: FPN predicts small objects at the high-resolution ___ levels of the pyramid.
exercises.explain: Lower levels of the FPN pyramid retain high spatial resolution, which is needed to localize small objects.

exercises.prompt: In a detector using FPN, where are large objects typically predicted?

options.text: At the upper, low-resolution levels of the pyramid (like P5)
At the lower, high-resolution levels of the pyramid (like P2)
Only in the input preprocessing step

exercises.explain: Correct! Large objects don't need high resolution and are best captured by the semantic features in upper levels.

exercises.prompt: You are running YOLOv8 with FPN. The model is outputting duplicate boxes for a medium-sized car from both the P3 and P4 levels. How is this resolved?

options.text: Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) gathers predictions from all levels and deletes the overlapping duplicate boxes.
The model disables the P3 layer automatically.
It averages the weights of P3 and P4 on the fly.

exercises.explain: Exactly! NMS is applied globally across all pyramid level predictions, cleaning up duplicates across scales. Phase 8: Modern Object Detection is complete. Next up: Image Segmentation.
